issues_found: The split strategy describes a user-level split across all 12 months for train/validation/test, but later mentions training the RL model on months 1-6 offline and conducting a live A/B test on months 10-12. This creates a temporal mismatch in the train/test split. If the 'test set' from the initial data split (15% of observations across all 12 months) is used for the offline evaluation, and then a separate live A/B test is conducted on months 10-12, the evaluation of the RL model is not consistent. A live A/B test implies real-time interaction and measurement from a designated 'test' period (months 10-12 in this case), not a pre-split historical data set.

The rule-based baseline is optimized on the validation set (months 7-9) using the 'same feature set as the RL model'. This implies that the rule-based system also uses features like 'user history embeddings' or 'user genre preference distribution', which are typically generated or learned as part of a more sophisticated model. If the rule-based system effectively incorporates such sophisticated features and is optimized for watch time, it might blur the distinction of whether the RL's performance gain is truly from its 'adaptive capability' or from better feature engineering that could also benefit a rule-based system.

The primary metric, 'average watch time per session', might not fully capture the 'increasing user engagement' hypothesis if sessions are very short but frequent due to better recommendations. While watching longer within a session is good, an increase in session *frequency* or *return rate* (a secondary metric) might be a more holistic measure of engagement. Relying solely on watch time per session could miss other important aspects of engagement like habit formation.

The experiment proposes to conduct a live A/B test during the test period (months 10-12). However, the RL model is trained offline on months 1-6. There's a risk that the offline-trained RL model might not perform optimally in a live online setting due to distribution shift or dynamics not captured in the historical data, especially if a significant amount of time has passed between training data and the live test. While the data includes seasonal cycles, user preferences can drift more rapidly than seasonal shifts alone account for.

The document states that 'Categorical features are one-hot encoded; missing values are imputed as 'unknown''. While 'unknown' is a common imputation strategy, it can sometimes introduce bias or an artificial category if the missingness is not random or if 'unknown' becomes a strong predictor due to data issues rather than true user behavior.
smoke_response.reasoning: The experiment design presents a good foundation but has critical inconsistencies in its train/test split and evaluation methodology that could compromise the validity of its conclusions regarding the RL model's true performance in a live environment.
